title: Intrinsic responsive grids

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Intrinsic responsive grids; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Intrinsic responsive grids
sources.claim: Responsive grid changes track count and size from available space and content needs rather than duplicating markup for each viewport.

sources.locator: MDN CSS Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Intrinsic responsive grids
sources.claim: Responsive grid changes track count and size from available space and content needs rather than duplicating markup for each viewport.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Responsive grid changes track count and size from available space and content needs rather than duplicating markup for each viewport.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A gallery collapses naturally to one column when its minimum card size no longer fits twice. Your goal is to create a grid that adapts across content and container widths without fixed device assumptions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Normal flow and display; Intrinsic sizing and constraints; Grid tracks and placement; Flex axes and sizing. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Responsive grid changes track count and size from available space and content needs rather than duplicating markup for each viewport. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Auto-fit and auto-fill differ for empty tracks, fixed minimums can overflow, and visual rearrangement must not contradict focus order. This draft therefore rejects: A responsive grid requires a media query for every possible device width.

includes: Combine flexible tracks, auto placement and content constraints before adding breakpoints.
Create a grid that adapts across content and container widths without fixed device assumptions.
excludes: Auto-fit and auto-fill differ for empty tracks, fixed minimums can overflow, and visual rearrangement must not contradict focus order.

outcomes: Create a grid that adapts across content and container widths without fixed device assumptions.

examples.title: Intrinsic responsive grids: concrete case
examples.situation: A gallery collapses naturally to one column when its minimum card size no longer fits twice.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A gallery collapses naturally to one column when its minimum card size no longer fits twice.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use content-aware track functions where suitable and add breakpoints only when the layout itself needs a structural change.
examples.result: The result follows because Use content-aware track functions where suitable and add breakpoints only when the layout itself needs a structural change.

examples.title: Intrinsic responsive grids: boundary case
examples.situation: Auto-fit and auto-fill differ for empty tracks, fixed minimums can overflow, and visual rearrangement must not contradict focus order.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Auto-fit and auto-fill differ for empty tracks, fixed minimums can overflow, and visual rearrangement must not contradict focus order.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: A responsive grid requires a media query for every possible device width.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Use content-aware track functions where suitable and add breakpoints only when the layout itself needs a structural change.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: A responsive grid requires a media query for every possible device width.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Intrinsic responsive grids, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Intrinsic responsive grids before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.

recap.summary: You should now be able to create a grid that adapts across content and container widths without fixed device assumptions. Keep this boundary in view: Auto-fit and auto-fill differ for empty tracks, fixed minimums can overflow, and visual rearrangement must not contradict focus order.
